policy_statement: By 2025, SFO will expand its carbon mitigation and offset program to include soil carbon sequestration projects where viable (HE.7-4).
mechanism_description: The program expansion includes soil carbon sequestration offset projects, increasing carbon stored in soils and lowering net greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Land management change → ↑ soil organic carbon (carbon sink) → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂ / ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Implement regenerative/agricultural soil management practices through eligible offset projects (e.g., cover cropping, reduced tillage, compost amendments, improved grazing) and finance/credit them as offsets. (2) Mediating process: Increased biomass inputs and reduced soil carbon losses raise soil organic carbon stocks (biogeochemical sequestration). (3) Outcome: Net carbon removal (or avoided soil carbon loss) credited as offsets, reducing the purchaser’s net reported GHG emissions and—if additional and durable—reducing atmospheric CO₂ over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased carbon stored in soils that removes CO₂ from the atmosphere and lowers net emissions via credited offsets.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: enhancing an ecosystem carbon sink (soil) to remove/store carbon → lower net GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the intent is “mitigation,” the mechanism operates through nature-based carbon sequestration (ecosystem enhancement), which maps best to Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a “soil carbon” activity were purely an efficiency or emissions-reduction measure (e.g., reduced fertilizer N₂O) without meaningful sequestration, classification could shift; but this canonical mechanism explicitly centers on soil carbon sequestration offsets (sink enhancement).  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using “soil carbon sequestration offsets” relies on increasing soil carbon stocks as the main climate effect, so NBS remains primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., agriculture practice type, offset quality provisions) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Offset integrity can affect real-world mitigation (additionality, permanence, leakage, reversals); however, the dominant mechanism remains nature-based carbon removal via increased soil carbon storage.
key_indicators: "expand its carbon mitigation and offset program"; "include soil carbon sequestration projects"; "By 2025"; "where viable"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved soil health, water retention, biodiversity/habitat, erosion control, farm resilience. (Not specified in text.)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement approach (purchase of offsets/credits), but the text frames it as expanding a program rather than specifying purchasing requirements or contracting terms; classified as incentive-program accordingly.
